creator: wbe@psr.com
text:
[Filed as "Security" because a random number generator that isn't random may be a risk if used for cryptography.  Feel free to reclassify.]

Summary: arc4random() observed producing heavily skewed results.

Affects:
* periodic's sleep times not being spread out may affect peak load on your servers.
* arc4random() not being random may be a cryptographic security issue.

Observed issue:
* /etc/defaults/periodic.conf attempts to distribute execution times using
  "sleep `jot -r 1 0 ${anticongestion_sleeptime}`" [whose value is 3600].

* I retrieved the source code for jot.c and verified that it calls arc4random()
   in the case of "jot -r 1 0 3600".  jot itself appears to be working properly
   since (double)divisor was 0x100000000, and the range was 3601.

* Actual sleep delays, however, are highly skewed.
   periodic emails its results after the above call to sleep.
   I took the mail submission times over the last 360 days and truncated the
   times to 60 1-minute bins.  If jot's results had been random over the
   3600 second range, the 60 bins would have roughly 6 entries each.
   Instead, the results are:

bin 0: 166  (46.1%)
bin 2:  14
bin 1:  13
bin 3:  10
-----------
Total: 203 of the 360 days, or 56.4% of the time.

The rest:
 2 bins had 6. (26 and 10)
 7 bins had 5.
 8 bins had 4.
17 bins had 3.
 6 bins had 2.
15 bins had 1.
 2 bins had 0. (47 and 53)

I looked briefly at the source for arc4random(), but didn't immediately spot anything that screamed "same value every day at 03:01" or the like (getmicrouptime() seemed reasonable for seeding to my untrained eye).

Configuration:
  AMD64, 6 core CPU;
  FreeBSD 13.0-RELEASE (but this has been going on for years, under FreeBSD 9, 10, 11, and 12, too)
      kernel: random: unblocking device
      kernel: random: entropy device external interface
  the system is often quiescent that time of morning;
  the system is seldom rebooted, so getmicrouptime() increases 86400sec/day.

 HTH,
 -WBE
